summary: The author decomposed the competition task into two subproblems: efs classification and efs_time regression, leveraging knowledge that the dataset is synthetic. They trained multiple tree-based models for both tasks, using a trick of fixing efs=1 during regression inference to estimate time given event occurrence. Predictions were combined into risk scores and further refined by a custom neural network trained with a metric-approximating loss. The final submission was a weighted ensemble of these pipelines, with additional experiments on AutoGluon and post-inference calibration.
extensive_summary:
## Problem Decomposition
Leveraging prior knowledge that the data is synthetic (referencing the SurvivalGAN paper), the authors split the task into two distinct components: efs classification and efs_time regression. For the regression component, XGBoost and HistGBM were trained with efs as an extra feature. During inference on validation and test sets, they fixed efs=1 to estimate the time until efs occurs. For classification, RealMLP, HistGBM, and CatBoost were trained without any feature engineering.

## Prediction Combination & Risk Scores
The two outputs were combined using the formula R = p(efs=1) * p(efs_time | efs=1), where the conditional time probability was derived via a sigmoid transformation of the regression predictions. This approach effectively converts the regression output into a risk score that accounts for both event occurrence and timing.

## Custom Neural Network & Loss
To further improve performance, a custom neural network was trained to approximate the competition metric directly and predict risk scores. This network took regression predictions from the tree models as input and was optimized using a custom loss function that combined a ranking-based metric approximation with an auxiliary binary classification loss. The implementation filters predictions based on a threshold and applies a tanh transformation to the risk differences.

## Ensembling & Post-Processing
The final submission was constructed by rank-ensembling the main pipeline with the neural network pipeline, with ensemble weights tuned on cross-validation out-of-fold (OOF) predictions. The authors also explored automated machine learning via AutoGluon, demonstrating that splitting the problem allowed even medium-quality AutoGluon settings to achieve gold-medal-level scores. Additionally, they experimented with post-processing calibration using a custom sigmoid function, which improved OOF scores but yielded mixed results on the public leaderboard.
competition_flow: Raw data is split into efs classification and efs_time regression tasks; tree models and a custom NN are trained on these tasks; predictions are combined into risk scores using a derived formula; outputs are rank-ensembled with CV-tuned weights and optionally calibrated before submission.
loss_functions: custom ranking loss approximating competition metric
cv_strategy: Cross-validation with out-of-fold (OOF) predictions used for tuning ensemble weights and calibration parameters.
ensembling: Weighted ensemble of tree models tuned on CV, rank-ensembled with a custom neural network pipeline, followed by optional sigmoid calibration on OOF predictions.
insights: Decomposing a complex metric into classification and regression subtasks can simplify modeling and improve performance.
Fixing a binary feature to 1 during regression inference effectively estimates conditional time given event occurrence.
Training a neural network to directly approximate the competition metric can yield better risk score calibration than standard losses.
critical_findings: Post-processing calibration improved OOF scores but slightly decreased the public leaderboard score, highlighting the risk of overfitting to validation metrics.
Splitting the problem into two tasks made it possible to achieve gold-medal-level scores using AutoGluon's medium quality setting.
